narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which looks at key indicators from past students' paths to the bar exam. Your profile includes a Year 3 school decile of 2, a Year 1 decile of 3, an LSAT score of 34, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, and plans to attend full-time, which is the typical choice for most students. The model predicts a 65.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection to ensure we admit those most likely to succeed. The biggest factor pushing this prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking, which is notably lower than the average of around the 5th or 6th decile—suggesting your later academic performance in school raised concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law. Your LSAT score of 34, while solid, fell short of the typical 36.7 average and worked somewhat against you, though not as strongly as your Year 3 performance. In contrast, your Year 1 decile, undergraduate GPA, family income bracket, and full-time status each nudged the prediction slightly toward success, aligning closer to or better than averages in those areas. Compared to typical admitted students, your early grades and background are competitive, but the drop-off in Year 3 stood out as the dominant signal of risk. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive family—many in similar situations thrive with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, aim to retake the LSAT for a higher score, and consider part-time options if they fit your goals better. Building a narrative of consistent improvement in your next application could shift the model's view. We're rooting for you to strengthen these areas and reapply stronger. Keep pursuing your passion for law—you've got this.
